title: Herbal antibacterial surface spray
subtitle: A washing-soda-and-thyme spray for worktops, sinks, and tiles
excerpt: Washing soda provides the alkaline cleaning power; thyme and tea tree essential oils contribute antimicrobial activity. The result is a surface spray that cuts through grease and leaves a clean herbal scent. Safe on sealed stone, glass, and ceramic -- not for natural marble or granite.
sourceNotes: Antimicrobial properties of Thymus vulgaris essential oil: Dorman and Deans, Journal of Applied Microbiology (2000). Tea tree (Melaleuca alternifolia) activity: Carson et al., Clinical Microbiology Reviews (2006). Washing soda alkalinity and grease-cutting: basic soapmaking and cleaning chemistry referenced in cleaning handbooks.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale up proportionally. Keep each bottle to 500 ml so the concentration stays correct.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a fresh bottle every month; the tea tree oil oxidises over time and loses potency.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use within one month. Discard if the spray develops cloudiness, a sour or rancid smell, or if the essential oil separates and no longer disperses on shaking. Shake before each use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sodium carbonate -- a stronger alkali than bicarbonate of soda. Cuts grease and lifts mineral deposits from surfaces. Do not use on skin in concentrated form.

content.text: This spray combines the alkaline degreasing power of

with thyme and tea tree essential oils, both of which have well-documented antimicrobial properties. One bottle lasts around two to three weeks of daily kitchen use. The formula keeps one month before the essential oils begin to oxidise.

items.prepNote: warm, to dissolve the washing soda

content.text: Measure 470 ml of warm distilled water into the spray bottle or a measuring jug. Add the 10 g of washing soda and stir or swirl until fully dissolved. Washing soda granules take around 30 seconds to dissolve in warm water; cold water takes longer.

Add 20 drops of tea tree essential oil and, if using, 10 drops of lavender. Essential oils do not mix with water on their own; they disperse temporarily when the bottle is shaken. Shake before each use.

Pour into a 500 ml spray bottle with a trigger head. Label with the contents and the date. The amber glass or HDPE plastic bottles used for room sprays both work well; avoid soft plastic, which can degrade with prolonged essential-oil contact.

Shake well, spray the surface, and wipe with a damp cloth or sponge. For heavy grease, leave on the surface for two minutes before wiping. Rinse food-preparation surfaces with plain water after use, particularly where the washing-soda concentration might leave a faint residue on cut surfaces.

Works on sealed stone, ceramic tiles, stainless steel, glass, enamelled surfaces, and painted walls. Do not use on natural marble, limestone, or travertine (washing soda is alkaline; these surfaces prefer pH-neutral cleaners). Do not use on unsealed wood or on electronics.

Keep at room temperature, out of direct sunlight. Use within one month. Discard if the spray develops cloudiness, a sour or rancid smell, or if the essential oils no longer re-disperse on shaking. Essential oils fade with oxidation; a spray that has lost its scent has also lost most of its antimicrobial activity.
